Implementing this in C++.

But wait, in the code, the function is supposed to return a long long.

So, in the code:

#include <vector>
#include <algorithm> // for max and min functions
using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    long long maximumTripletValue(vector<int>& nums) {
        int n = nums.size();
        if (n < 3) return 0; // though constraints say n >=3.

        vector<long long> left_max(n, LLONG_MIN);
        vector<long long> left_min(n, LLONG_MAX);

        for (int j = 1; j < n; j++) {
            for (int i = 0; i < j; i++) {
                long long diff = nums[i] - nums[j];
                if (diff > left_max[j]) {
                    left_max[j] = diff;
                }
                if (diff < left_min[j]) {
                    left_min[j] = diff;
                }
            }
        }

        vector<long long> right_max(n, LLONG_MIN);
        vector<long long> right_min(n, LLONG_MAX);

        for (int j = n-2; j >= 0; j--) {
            right_max[j] = max(nums[j+1], right_max[j+1]);
            right_min[j] = min(nums[j+1], right_min[j+1]);
        }

        long long max_val = 0;

        for (int j = 1; j < n-1; j++) {
            long long a = left_max[j] * right_max[j];
            long long b = left_max[j] * right_min[j];
            long long c = left_min[j] * right_max[j];
            long long d = left_min[j] * right_min[j];

            long long current_max = max({a, b, c, d});
            if (current_max > max_val) {
                max_val = current_max;
            }
        }

        return max_val > 0 ? max_val : 0;
    }
};

Wait, but in the code, for j in 1 to n-2.

Wait, in the loop for j, it's written as for (int j = 1; j < n-1; j++). Because n-1 is the last index, j can be up to n-2.

Yes.

But wait, in the code, the right_max and right_min are computed correctly.

The left_max and left_min are computed in O(n^2) time.

The right_max and right_min are computed in O(n) time.

Then, for each j, four products are computed, which is O(n) time.

So overall, the time complexity is O(n^2), which is acceptable for n=100.

Space complexity is O(n) for storing the left and right arrays.

Energy implications: since the algorithm is O(n^2), for n=100, it's manageable and energy efficient.

So, the code should work.

solution: 
```cpp
#include <vector>
#include <algorithm>
using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    long long maximumTripletValue(vector<int>& nums) {
        int n = nums.size();
        if (n < 3) return 0;

        vector<long long> left_max(n, LLONG_MIN);
        vector<long long> left_min(n, LLONG_MAX);

        for (int j = 1; j < n; j++) {
            for (int i = 0; i < j; i++) {
                long long diff = nums[i] - nums[j];
                if (diff > left_max[j]) {
                    left_max[j] = diff;
                }
                if (diff < left_min[j]) {
                    left_min[j] = diff;
                }
            }
        }

        vector<long long> right_max(n, LLONG_MIN);
        vector<long long> right_min(n, LLONG_MAX);

        for (int j = n - 2; j >= 0; j--) {
            right_max[j] = max(nums[j + 1], right_max[j + 1]);
            right_min[j] = min(nums[j + 1], right_min[j + 1]);
        }

        long long max_val = 0;

        for (int j = 1; j < n - 1; j++) {
            long long a = left_max[j] * right_max[j];
            long long b = left_max[j] * right_min[j];
            long long c = left_min[j] * right_max[j];
            long long d = left_min[j] * right_min[j];

            long long current_max = max({a, b, c, d});
            if (current_max > max_val) {
                max_val = current_max;
            }
        }

        return max_val > 0 ? max_val : 0;
    }
};
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n²)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution efficiently computes the maximum triplet value by precomputing maximum and minimum values for the left and right parts of each possible middle element, then evaluating all combinations to find the optimal product. This approach ensures we consider all potential triplet configurations without redundant calculations, making it both time and space efficient.
